title: Optimistic vs pessimistic locking, exactly 3 paragraphs
response:
Optimistic and pessimistic locking are two answers to the same problem you just hit — two writers reading the same row and the last one silently overwriting the first. Pessimistic locking takes an exclusive lock when you read (e.g. `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE`), so anyone else who wants that row blocks until your transaction commits or rolls back. Optimistic locking takes no lock at all: you read a version number along with the data, and your update runs as `UPDATE ... SET ..., version = version + 1 WHERE id = ? AND version = ?` — if zero rows match, someone changed it first and you must retry or surface an error.

Each fails differently. Pessimistic: a user opens an edit form that grabs a `FOR UPDATE` lock, then goes to lunch; every other request touching that row hangs until the transaction times out. Optimistic: two clerks both load inventory at version 7, both edit, the second save fails its version check and the clerk loses ten minutes of typing to a "please retry" error.

Rule of thumb: under low contention, go optimistic — conflicts are rare, so retries are cheap and you avoid lock overhead. Under high contention on hot rows, go pessimistic — constant retry storms cost more than briefly queuing writers, provided you keep transactions short.
